a. Selecting a pathogen. A pathogen is a microorganism which is capable of causing disease, particularly in the immune systems of mammals. Pathogens can be viruses, bacteria, protozoa, or fungi. Introduction

The human immune system is a complex and dynamic network of cells and molecules that work together to protect the body from infection. The immune system is constantly monitoring the body for potential threats, such as pathogens, and can rapidly respond to eliminate them. However, sometimes the immune system can mistakenly identify healthy cells and tissues as a threat, leading to autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, and lupus. Genetic modification of pathogens to target the human immune system could potentially be used to develop new treatments for autoimmune diseases.

Introduction

1.1. The human immune system

The human immune system is a complex system of cells, tissues, organs and processes that work together to defend the body against attack by foreign bodies (invaders) such as viruses and bacteria. It has two main components: innate immunity and adaptive immunity. The innate immune system provides a general defense against foreign invaders. It consists of barriers that prevent pathogens from entering the body, as well as phagocytes (white blood cells) that engulf and digest foreign material. The adaptive immune system is specific to the pathogen it is fighting. It consists of lymphocytes (white blood cells) that recognize and attack specific antigens. B-lymphocytes (B-cells) produce antibodies that attach to antigens and mark them for destruction by phagocytes. T-lymphocytes (T-cells) destroy infected cells or release chemicals that help fight the infection.
